# RFQ grid → AG Grid Server-Side Row Model (SSRM) — migration design

**Status:** PROPOSED DESIGN — not yet implemented. Written 2026-06-23, updated 2026-07-07 for
developer review.
**Scope:** cross-repo (`designflow-backend` + `designflow-frontend`).
**Decisions already made by product owner** (see "Decisions" below) — open questions for the
reviewer are listed at the end.

> **Prime directive (read first):** This migration is **purely additive**. Do **not** change or
> remove any existing RFQ behavior to make SSRM fit. Every current feature — the vendor detail
> sub-grid, Saved Views, Division logic, default Step filter, master/detail height, cell notes,
> selection/bulk ops, the pricing math — must behave **identically** before and after. The way we
> guarantee that is **Phase 0**: write characterization/regression tests for today's behavior
> *first*, keep the whole existing suite green, and prove the new endpoint returns byte-for-byte
> equivalent row data (especially the per-row `Vendors[]` array) before any UI switches over. See
> "Phase 0 gate" below — it is a hard gate, not a suggestion.

---

## Context / why

The RFQ list (`designflow-backend` `Customer.getAll` → frontend `rfq.component.ts`) currently
loads **every** RFQ row (3000+) in one unbounded `findAll` across ~10 joined tables, then does
all filtering/sorting **client-side**. It is the heaviest page in the app and the largest
page-load cost. The goal is a true SSRM migration: the backend paginates and applies
filtering + sorting + grouping **in SQL**, so they operate across **all** matching rows, not
just the rows currently displayed.

> ⚠️ This is the most critical page in the app. The plan ships behind a **dual-endpoint** so the
> existing `/getAllItem` remains an instant rollback path.

### SSRM is already proven elsewhere in this app (but not the grouped-with-detail variant)

SSRM is **not new** to DesignFlow — it is the established pattern for the other heavy grids, and
this migration should copy them, not invent anything:

| Grid | Row model | Master/detail? | Row grouping? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `itemLibrary` | **SSRM** (`rowModelType: 'serverSide'`) | no | flat |
| `prod_tracking` | **SSRM** | **yes** (`masterDetail: true` + `getDetailRowData`) | flat |
| `licensing_tracking`, `art-piece`, `sample_tracking` | **SSRM** | — | flat |
| **`rfq` (this page)** | **client-side** (all 3000+ rows in browser) | **yes** (`Vendors[]`) | **grouped** by RFQ Group |

So two of the three ingredients already ship: SSRM alone (`itemLibrary`) and **SSRM + master/detail
together** (`prod_tracking`). What RFQ adds on top — and what has **no in-repo precedent** — is
**server-side row *grouping* combined with master/detail**. That combination, plus the vendor
sub-grid specifics below, is exactly why RFQ was left for last.

### Why RFQ was deferred — the vendor detail sub-grid (the investigation)

The blocker everyone remembers as "something about the inner AG Grid" is concrete. The RFQ detail
sub-grid (factory quotes) is fed from the **inline `Vendors[]` association materialized on every
master row** — `getDetailRowData: params => params.successCallback(params.data.Vendors || [])`
(`rfq.component.ts`). That single design fact creates four coupled risks that flat SSRM grids
never face:

1. **Paginating a `hasMany` fan-out truncates the sub-grid data.** A leaf RFQ item has N vendor
   quotes. A naive `LIMIT/OFFSET` `findAll` with the `RFQVendors` join paginates the *flattened*
   item×vendor rows, so a page boundary can slice a row's `Vendors[]` in half or drop items
   entirely. The fix is `subQuery: true` (LIMIT applies to RFQItems, not the join) — get this
   wrong and detail sub-grids render **partial or empty**.
2. **We have already been burned here.** A prior `develop` "RFQ performance" refactor switched
   vendor loading to **lazy per-row** (`getRfqItemVendors`); users saw **empty detail sub-grids**
   while the `Res` count still showed quotes. It was a frontend↔backend data-contract break and
   was **reverted** (see `AGENTS.md` → "2026-06-15 — empty RFQ sub-grids after deploy"). SSRM must
   keep delivering the **full `Vendors[]` inline on each leaf** (via `utility.remapRFQItem`) — it
   must **not** reintroduce lazy vendor loading.
3. **The detail panel height is hand-computed up front from `Vendors.length`.** `getRowHeight()`
   reserves `Vendors.length * rowHeight + headerHeight + offset` because AG Grid needs a
   master-detail row's pixel height *before* it renders (it can't auto-measure the Angular detail
   component) — see `AGENTS.md` → "Master-detail panel height is hand-computed". If a leaf arrives
   without its complete `Vendors[]`, rows clip or collapse.
4. **Many unrelated features read `node.data.Vendors` directly** — the Select-Factories
   duplicate-factory guard (`existingVendorIds = node.data.Vendors.map(v => v?.Vendor?.id)`), the
   computed **Res** column, the `req_status` detail-row class rules, and the requote-flag
   highlight. The guard is **UI-only** (no DB/backend constraint) and casing-sensitive (capital
   `Vendors`). Any change that drops, reshapes, or lazy-loads that array **silently disables** the
   guard and miscomputes Res.

**Conclusion:** the vendor sub-grid is safe to migrate, but only if the new server pages deliver
each leaf's `Vendors[]` **exactly** as today's `getAll` does. That equivalence is not something to
eyeball at the end — it is what Phase 0 exists to lock down before any code switches over.

## Phase 0 gate — characterization tests BEFORE any SSRM code (mandatory)

**Nothing in the backend or frontend switches to the paged endpoint until Phase 0 is green.** The
goal: capture today's behavior as executable tests, then prove the new path reproduces it.

1. **Characterize the current row contract (backend).** Add a test that calls the existing
   `Customer.getAll` path against a seeded fixture and snapshots the shape of the returned rows —
   with explicit assertions that **every row carries a complete `Vendors[]` array**, each element
   shaped `{ Vendor: { id }, Price, Note, Status, req_status, quote_id, ... }`, and that `Res`
   (responded/requested) matches the current JS computation. This snapshot is the **contract** the
   paged/export endpoints must satisfy.
2. **Equivalence test across both endpoints.** When `getAllPaged` / `exportAll` exist, a test
   drives the **same** filters/sorts through both the legacy `getAll` and the new endpoints and
   asserts the **union of paged rows == legacy rows** for the same query (same ids, same
   `Vendors[]` per row, same order once grouping/tiebreak is accounted for). This is the single
   most important test — it is what proves "nothing broke."
3. **Characterize the current UI behavior (frontend).** Before touching `rfq.component.ts`, add/con­firm
   regression specs in `rfq.component.spec.ts` that pin the behaviors most at risk from the row
   model change: detail sub-grid receives `params.data.Vendors` and renders quote rows; the
   duplicate-factory guard reads `node.data.Vendors`; `getRowHeight()` reserves height from
   `Vendors.length`; default Step filter applies; Division switch refreshes; Saved Views restore;
   cell notes merge. (Note the pre-existing `notesDataSource` type error in that spec — leave it,
   it is unrelated.)
4. **Green-before-green-after rule.** The **entire** existing frontend (`yarn test`) and backend
   (`npm test`) suites must be **green before Phase 1 starts and green after every phase**. A red
   suite at any point halts the migration. New tests are added; **no existing test is weakened,
   skipped, or deleted** to make SSRM pass — if an existing test would fail under SSRM, that is a
   behavior change and must be fixed in the migration, not in the test.

Only when 1–4 hold does implementation proceed to the backend/frontend phases below. Treat the
dual-endpoint (legacy `/getAllItem` untouched) as the rollback lever the whole time.

## Decisions (from product owner)

1. **Keep server-side row grouping** by "RFQ Group" (collapsible groups, ordered by most-recent
   child "Date Modified", matching today's `postSortRows`).
2. **"Res" and merch-group columns MUST be filterable** (and Res sortable). **Carton
   length/width/height** do NOT need sort/filter.
3. **Export must return the full filtered set** (server-side), not just loaded pages.

## Why this is non-trivial (the core challenges)

- **Grouping.** The RFQ grid is grouped by RFQ Group by default. SSRM server-side grouping means
  the backend must answer two kinds of requests: the **group level** (return one row per RFQ
  Group) and the **leaf level** (return the rows inside an expanded group). The other SSRM grids
  in this app (prod_tracking, itemLibrary) are flat, so there is no in-repo precedent for the
  grouped variant.
- **Computed columns.** SSRM pushes sort/filter to SQL, but several columns are computed in JS
  with no DB column: **Res** (responded/requested count), **carton dims** (from the chosen
  vendor), and the **merch-group columns** (Group/Material/Treatment/Construction/Size store an
  *id*; the display name comes from an in-memory lookup). Res + merch-groups must be made
  filterable via SQL; carton dims will simply have sort/filter disabled.
- **Cross-cutting features** that must keep working: master/detail vendor grid, Saved Views,
  Division logic, the default Step filter, highlighted-item, cell notes, and selection/bulk ops.

---

## Backend design (`designflow-backend`)

All new query logic goes in **`helpers/rfqListQuery.js`** (extend; keep the existing exports that
`Customer.getAll` still uses). New thin model methods + controller + routes. **`Customer.getAll`
is left untouched** for rollback.

### 1. Shared base condition
Extract the existing internal-vs-vendor `where` construction out of `getAll` into
`buildBaseCondition({ user, body })` — division/archive/active filters, the vendor
`$RFQVendors.*$` branch, the `filterd_rfq`/highlighted OR-branch. Pure function, reused by both
the old and new paths so behavior cannot drift.

### 2. Field map + filter/sort translators
- `RFQ_FIELD_MAP`: colId → `{ col | assoc+col, type }`, type ∈ `text | date | numString | lookup
  | merch | res`:
  - direct cols: Price Code (`rfqItem_style_number`), Description (`rfqItem_description`), Date
    Modified (`rfqItem_date_modified`), Created Date, Factory_updated.
  - `numString` (TEXT columns needing numeric CAST): FOB Cost (`rfqItem_fob_cost`), Landed Cost
    (`rfqItem_landed_cost`), CBM/pc (`rfqItem_cbm_per_price`), Quantity (`rfqItem_quantity`).
  - `lookup` (filter/sort on the joined name): Status (`RFQItemStatus.RFQItemStatus_title`),
    Step, Customer, Chosen Vendor (`Factory.factory_nickname`), Depth, Delivery Location,
    RFQ Group.
  - `merch`: Group/Material/Treatment/Construction → `rfqItem_udf1..4`; Size → `rfqItem_size_l_w`.
  - `res`: correlated subquery (see §3).
- `translateFilterModel(filterModel, { merchNameToIds, schemaPrefix })` → Sequelize `where`
  fragment. Handles:
  - **agMultiColumnFilter**: normalize to the first non-null sub-model.
  - **agTextColumnFilter**: contains/equals/startsWith/endsWith/notContains/blank → `Op.iLike`
    (lookups key on `$Assoc.col$`).
  - **agSetColumnFilter**: `{ values }` → `Op.in` (handle `(blank)`→null via `Op.or`).
  - **agDateColumnFilter**: equals (day-range)/greaterThan/lessThan/inRange → `gte`/`lte`/`between`.
  - **numString**: regex-guarded CAST literal to avoid `invalid input syntax` on non-numeric/empty
    text: `("col" ~ '^[0-9.]+$') AND CAST("col" AS double precision) <op> <n>`.
- `translateSortModel(sortModel, { schemaPrefix })` → Sequelize `order`: numString uses
  `CAST(NULLIF("col",'') AS double precision) <dir> NULLS LAST`; lookups order on `$Assoc.col$`;
  Res orders on the correlated subquery; always append `rfqItem_id DESC` as a deterministic
  tiebreak.

### 3. Res (computed; no DB column)
`buildResSubqueries(schemaPrefix)` returns correlated scalar subqueries over `RFQVendor`,
correlated on `RFQItem.rfqItem_id`, mirroring the JS in `helpers/utility.js`:
- requested = `COUNT(DISTINCT vendor_id_fk)` where `COALESCE(req_status,0) <> 1`
- responded = same + `RFQVendor_amount ~ '^[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?$'`

Usage:
- **WHERE (filter):** parse the typed term `"r/q"` (also `"3/"`, `"/5"`); for `contains`, match
  `(responded || '/' || requested) ILIKE '%term%'`.
- **ORDER:** `(responded) <dir>, (requested) <dir>`.

All raw literals MUST use the `process.env.schema` prefix idiom already used by the
`getRFQGroup` recency subquery in `lib.model.js`.

### 4. Merch-group filter (names → ids, no join)
`buildMerchNameToIds(merchGroupData)` inverts the cached merch-group catalog
(`getCachedMerchGroup`) to `lowercased mg_desc → [mg_id...]`. Then:
- **set filter** (`values: ['Cotton', ...]`): map each value to its ids, flatten →
  `{ [col]: { [Op.in]: ids } }`; unknown name → `IN [-1]` (no rows); `(blank)` → `Op.or` with
  `null`.
- **text contains**: scan the cached descs for the substring, collect ids → `IN (ids)`.

Cheap because the merch catalog is small and already cached (5-min TTL). Carton dims get no
translator entry — sort/filter is disabled on those columns in the frontend config.

### 5. Group-level query (`buildGroupLevelQuery`)
When `groupKeys.length === 0` (top level): aggregate **from `RFQItem`** (NOT `RFQGroup.findAll`,
so empty/non-matching groups disappear):
- `group by RFQGroup_id, RFQGroup_name`; `MAX(rfqItem_date_modified)` for recency
  `ORDER BY ... DESC NULLS LAST`; `limit/offset`; apply base condition + translated filter.
- `lastRow` via a separate `count({ distinct:true, col:'rfqItem_rfq_group' })` (NEVER
  `findAndCountAll` with `group` — it returns per-group arrays, not a scalar).
- Rows: `{ "RFQ Group": name, childCount, lastActivity }`.

### 6. Leaf-level query (`buildLeafLevelQuery`)
When inside a group: the existing findAll shape (`attributes: { exclude:
RFQ_LIST_ITEM_EXCLUDE_ATTRIBUTES }`, `include: buildRfqListLookupIncludes(...)`), plus:
- `'$RFQGroup.RFQGroup_name$' = groupKeys[0]`, translated filter/sort, `limit/offset`.
- **`subQuery: true`** (so LIMIT applies to RFQItems, not the `RFQVendors` hasMany fan-out).
- `lastRow` via `count({ distinct:true, col:'rfqItem_id' })`.
- Rows pass through the **existing `utility.remapRFQItem`** unchanged → each leaf keeps its
  `Vendors` array (master/detail keeps working) and `Res`.
- Vendor role uses the leaf builder **without** grouping (grouping is an internal-role feature);
  keep the `archiveStaleVendorQuotes` call and vendor email backfill from `getAll`.

### 7. Model + controller + routes
- `Customer.getAllPaged` — read `rowGroupCols`/`groupKeys`/`sortModel`/`filterModel`/`startRow`/
  `endRow` + existing body fields; pick group vs leaf level; `Promise.all`(page, count, caches);
  return `{ rows, lastRow }`.
- `Customer.exportAll` — leaf builder with **no limit/offset**, flat across all groups, same
  translators → full `remapRFQItem` array.
- `controllers/main.controller.js`: `findAllPaged`, `exportAll` (new `SYS-###` codes).
- `routes/lib.router.js`: `POST /getAllItemPaged` and `POST /getAllItemExport`, same
  `authRole([...])` as `/getAllItem`. Leave `/getAllItem` intact.

---

## Frontend design (`designflow-frontend`)

Mirror the proven SSRM pattern in `pages/prod_tracking` (already `masterDetail:true` + SSRM) and
`pages/itemLibrary`.

- **`rfq.component.ts` gridOptions**: add `rowModelType:'serverSide'`, `cacheBlockSize:100`,
  `maxBlocksInCache:10`, `blockLoadDebounceMillis:500`, `serverSideEnableClientSideSort:false`.
  Keep `masterDetail:true` + `getDetailRowData: p => p.successCallback(p.data.Vendors||[])`.
  Keep `rowGroup:true` on RFQ Group; set the group key to the **string** `RFQGroup_name`
  (`keyCreator`/`getServerSideGroupKey`) so `groupKeys[0]` round-trips. **Remove the client
  `postSortRows` + autoGroupColumnDef recency comparator** (recency now comes from server group
  order). `getRowId`: leaves → `data.id` (rfqItem_id); guard group rows.
- **Datasource** (`createServerSideDatasource`): `getRows` posts `{ startRow, endRow,
  rowGroupCols, groupKeys, sortModel, filterModel, divisionCode, divisionStatus, status,
  highlightedItem, filterd_rfq }` to `/getAllItemPaged`, then
  `params.success({ rowData: res.rows, rowCount: res.lastRow })`; merge cell notes per page
  (`mergePreloadCellNotes`). Replace `getAllRFQItem()` → `setGridOption('rowData', …)` with
  `setGridOption('serverSideDatasource', …)`. Division/status/highlight changes call
  `gridApi.refreshServerSide({ purge:true })` instead of refetching the whole array.
- **Service** (`main.service.ts`): `getAllItemPaged(request)` + `getAllItemExport(request)`
  (POST, NOT cached).
- **Column config** (`rfq.grid.config.ts`): carton length/width/height → `sortable:false,
  filter:false`. Res + merch-group columns stay filterable (now server-backed).
- **Default Step filter**: keep `applyDefaultStepFilter` (sets a `step` set-filter model) — under
  SSRM it is sent to the server and handled by `translateFilterModel`.
- **Export** (`onBtExport`): call `getAllItemExport` with the current filter/sort model; build the
  file from the returned full set (spinner while fetching).
- **Saved Views**: column-state restore unaffected; filter-model restore now triggers a server
  fetch — fine.
- **Selection / bulk ops** (Multi Quote, Add Factories, Reminder, bulk status): `selectAll:
  'filtered'` only covers loaded pages under SSRM — keep row-by-row selection; full-dataset
  "select all across all pages" is a known limitation (out of scope unless requested).

---

## Critical files

- `designflow-backend/helpers/rfqListQuery.js` — translators, field map, builders, Res, merch
- `designflow-backend/models/lib.model.js` — `getAllPaged`, `exportAll`; refactor base condition
- `designflow-backend/controllers/main.controller.js`, `routes/lib.router.js`
- `designflow-backend/helpers/utility.js` — `remapRFQItem` reused unchanged
- `designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts`
- `designflow-frontend/src/app/helpers/ag-grid/rfq-grid/rfq.grid.config.ts`
- `designflow-frontend/src/app/helpers/services/main.service.ts`
- `designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/rfq/detail-cell-renderer/...` (no change — reads `data.Vendors`)

## Tests / verification

> The **Phase 0 gate** above defines the tests that must exist and pass *before* implementation.
> This section lists the *additional* per-phase tests. The rule stands throughout: **every existing
> test stays green before and after each phase**, and no existing test is weakened to accommodate
> SSRM.

- **Phase 0 (write first, see gate above):** current-row-contract snapshot (each row carries a
  complete `Vendors[]`, `Res` matches JS); legacy-vs-paged **equivalence** test (same query → same
  ids + same `Vendors[]` per row); frontend characterization specs for detail-grid `Vendors`
  feed, duplicate-factory guard, `getRowHeight` from `Vendors.length`, Step filter, Division
  refresh, Saved Views, cell notes.
- **Backend unit tests** (extend `tests/unit/rfqListQuery.test.js`, no DB): `translateFilterModel`
  (text/set/date/numString/multi/lookup), `translateSortModel` (CAST+NULLS, Res order, tiebreak),
  `buildResSubqueries` (with/without schema prefix), merch name→id (set/contains/unknown→[-1]),
  `buildBaseCondition` (internal vs vendor), `buildGroupLevelQuery`/`buildLeafLevelQuery` option
  shape. Keep the 4 existing tests green.
- **Backend equivalence test (the proof nothing broke):** for a seeded fixture, assert the union of
  `getAllPaged` leaf pages equals the legacy `getAll` result set for the same filter/sort —
  identical row ids and identical per-row `Vendors[]` (length, `Vendor.id`s, `req_status`, prices),
  and that `exportAll` returns that same full set flat. This is the gate that vendor sub-grids
  cannot silently regress.
- **Frontend unit tests**: a datasource spec (`getRows` builds the right request; calls
  `params.success({rowData,rowCount})` and `params.fail` on error) mirroring prod_tracking; carton
  cols non-sortable/filterable. Keep the Phase 0 characterization specs green unchanged.
- **End-to-end manual (the real proof — run the app):** group level returns recency-ordered
  groups; expanding a group lazy-loads leaves with vendor master/detail intact; sort/filter on
  Price Code, dates, prices (numeric), Status, Customer, **Res**, and **merch-group** columns act
  across the full dataset (verify with a value known to live on a non-first page); default Step
  filter applies; Division switch refreshes; Export downloads the full filtered set. Confirm via DB
  counts that filtering reduces totals (not just the page). Run backend `npm test` + frontend
  `yarn test` (note: `rfq.component.spec.ts` has a pre-existing `notesDataSource` type error,
  unrelated to this work).

## Key risks / Sequelize gotchas

- **Do not reintroduce lazy vendor loading.** Each leaf must return its **full inline `Vendors[]`**
  via `utility.remapRFQItem` (capital `V`, unchanged shape). A prior lazy per-row vendor refactor
  (`getRfqItemVendors`) produced empty detail sub-grids in production and was reverted — SSRM must
  page *items*, never lazy-load their vendors. The Phase 0 equivalence test guards this.
- `subQuery:true` required for correct LIMIT with the `RFQVendors` hasMany (otherwise the fan-out
  slices a row's `Vendors[]` across page boundaries); counts must use `distinct:true` + `col`;
  never `findAndCountAll` with `group`; CAST on free-text numeric columns must be regex-guarded;
  correlated Res subqueries and all raw literals must use the `process.env.schema` prefix.
- **Performance:** Res subqueries run per row — confirm/add indexes on `RFQVendor.RFQitem_id_fk`,
  `rfqItem_rfq_group`, `rfqItem_date_modified` (flag to DBA).
- Verify hardest: group↔leaf expansion, master/detail vendor data, and that filter/sort genuinely
  span all rows.

## Open questions for the reviewing developer

1. **Res filter semantics** — is the expected user input the `"responded/requested"` string
   (e.g. `"3/5"`, `"3/"`, `"/5"`), or do they think of it as two numbers / "has responses vs
   none"? This determines the WHERE parsing.
2. **Lookup sort under `subQuery:true`** — confirm ordering by joined names (Status/Customer/etc.)
   generates correct SQL with LIMIT still wrapping RFQItem; may need `Sequelize.literal` ordering.
3. **Group-level highlightedItem** — acceptable to bump only the leaf within its group (current
   behavior is a single-array bump), or must the containing group also jump to the top?
4. **Indexes** — are the three indexes above already present in the prod schema?
5. **Selection/bulk ops** — is loaded-pages-only selection acceptable, or is full-dataset
   "select all matching" required (extra work)?

{"cmd":"$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'
$repos = @('designflow-backend','designflow-bff','designflow-data-syncing','designflow-frontend','designflow-item-master','designflow-tracking')
foreach ($r in $repos) {
  Write-Host "=== $r ==="
  Push-Location "C:\repos\dflow plm\$r"
  git fetch origin
  $branch = git branch --show-current
  if ($branch -ne 'sandbox-albert') { throw "$r is on $branch, expected sandbox-albert" }
  git merge --no-edit origin/develop
  git push origin sandbox-albert
  git pull --ff-only origin sandbox-albert
  git status --short --branch
  Pop-Location
}","workdir":"C:\repos\dflow plm","shell":"powershell","yield_time_ms":30000,"max_output_tokens":60000}

# AGENTS.md — Designflow Backend: Primary Developer Guide

Read this file in full before touching any code. It is the authoritative orientation document for any developer or AI session working on this repository.

> **Supabase migration:** Cloud SQL → Supabase work is governed by the workspace-wide [`SUPABASE-MIGRATION.md`](SUPABASE-MIGRATION.md), the canonical copy of which lives in this repo. Read it before changing DB config, pooling, schema, or data-access patterns. This is the **first** backend service tested against Supabase; keep Sequelize and existing auth/business/file behavior intact.

---

## 1. What this service is

Designflow Backend is the main REST API for the Designflow PLM platform. It is a stateless Node.js + Express service backed by PostgreSQL (Sequelize ORM). Identity comes from a JWT on every request; session state does not exist. The service runs on Google Cloud Run and is reached exclusively through the BFF (`designflow-bff`), which terminates the user JWT and adds an OIDC service-to-service token before forwarding. The frontend is a separate Angular SPA in a sibling repository. For a full system diagram see `docs/architecture.md`.

---

## 2. Multi-model AI note

There is no universal ignore-file standard across AI coding tools.

`.claudeignore` works for Claude Code.

When using any other AI tool, paste this file as your first message and follow the instructions in the "What to ignore" section.

---

## 2a. Documentation map: what to read for each task

Always start with this file (`AGENTS.md`). Then load other docs **only when relevant** — do
not ingest every `.md`.

Cross-repo infrastructure standard: Designflow's current non-code runtime and
operating environment is tracked in
[u2giants/albert-standards `infrastructure/designflow-cloud-run.md`](https://github.com/u2giants/albert-standards/blob/main/infrastructure/designflow-cloud-run.md).
If a change affects Cloud Run topology, Cloud Build/deploy behavior, domains,
BFF routing, secrets/env handling, storage, auth/OIDC, or database operating
rules, update that standards page in the same docs pass.

| Task / question | Read these docs | Usually do not need |
|---|---|---|
| Quick orientation | `README.md`, `AGENTS.md` | deep `docs/` |
| Modify endpoints / model / domain logic | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/architecture.md` | `docs/deployment.md` unless deploy changes |
| Add/change config, env vars, secrets, app settings | `AGENTS.md` (§12), `docs/configuration.md`, `docs/deployment.md` if prod runtime affected | unrelated architecture docs |
| DB schema / migration / new column / data flow | `AGENTS.md` (§6 Decision tree, §8 Data model), `docs/architecture.md`, `docs/configuration.md` if env affected | `docs/deployment.md` unless rollout changes |
| Local setup, run, test, debug | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/development.md` | `docs/deployment.md` |
| Deploy / Docker / Cloud Build / Cloud Run / rollback | `AGENTS.md` (§13), `docs/deployment.md`, `docs/configuration.md` | `docs/development.md` |
| AI proxy (OpenRouter / DeepSeek), tariffs (Section 301 / Trump 2025), HTS | `AGENTS.md` (§3, §11), `docs/architecture.md` | local-only dev docs |
| MCP server work | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/mcp-integration.md` | unrelated module docs |
| Internal REST endpoint reference | `AGENTS.md`, `docs/api-reference.md`, `routes/lib.router.js` | deployment docs |
| Investigate a bug/incident | `AGENTS.md` (§14 Critical incident log), the area's doc, `HANDOFF.md` if present | unrelated docs |
| Continue unfinished work | `AGENTS.md`, `HANDOFF.md` if present, docs it names | docs outside the handoff scope |
| Claude Code session | `CLAUDE.md`, then `AGENTS.md` | other docs unless needed |
| Docs-only cleanup | `AGENTS.md`, `README.md`, affected `docs/` | source except to verify |

No backend `HANDOFF.md` is currently required. Update this table whenever a doc is added, removed, or repurposed.

---

## 3. Repository structure

| Path | What it is |
|---|---|
| `index.js` | Server entry — middleware stack (including the `x-user-authorization` → `authorization` swap) and `listen` |
| `config/env.js` | dotenv loader; resolves `.env.${NODE_ENV}` |
| `config/db.config.js` | PostgreSQL connection params; throws on missing required key |
| `config/digitalocean.config.js` | DigitalOcean Spaces credentials |
| `config/brevo.config.js` | Brevo email credentials |
| `helpers/auth.js` | JWT RS256 verify; `authRole(roles[])` middleware factory; `getUserInfo(token)` |
| `helpers/context.middleware.js` | `AsyncLocalStorage` per-request store `{ user, reqId }` — lets deep-stack code read the current user without prop-drilling |
| `helpers/openrouter.js` | OpenRouter API client; prompt-caching markers for anthropic/deepseek/google/qwen models |
| `helpers/deepseek.js` | DeepSeek direct API client (`api.deepseek.com`); maps `reasoning_effort: 'none'\|'high'\|'max'` to the DeepSeek `thinking` parameter; used when `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` is set and `api='deepseek-direct'` |
| `helpers/qwen.js` | Alibaba Qwen direct API client; Responses API session cache for `session_id` calls, Chat Completions explicit cache fallback for one-off calls; used when `DASHSCOPE_API_KEY` or `QWEN_API_KEY` is set and `api='qwen-direct'` |
| `helpers/cache-metrics.js` | Normalizes provider usage/cache telemetry into shared hit/miss/write/hit-rate fields |
| `services/ai-cache-telemetry.service.js` | Persists per-response AI cache telemetry into `ai_cache_events` and aggregates admin cache stats by provider/model/strategy |
| `helpers/section301.js` | Section 301 PDF text extraction + regex HTS parser (lazy-requires `pdf-parse`) |
| `helpers/hts-rates.js` | MFN base-rate lookup against bundled USITC HTS JSON (`data/hts-rates.json`) |
| `helpers/trump2025.js` | Trump 2025 reciprocal-tariff lookup; alias-aware country normalization |
| `helpers/factories.js` | Factory list / country map / `updateCountry(id, country)` |
| `helpers/digitalocean.js` | S3 client; presigned URL, ACL, upload, delete; `uploadBufferToSpaces(buffer, fileName, contentType)` for in-memory Buffer uploads (used by bulk import to avoid writing temp files) |
| `helpers/mention.js` | `parseMentions` / `resolveMentions` for @mention pipeline |
| `helpers/multer.js` | Multer instances: `multerGetThumb` (disk, thumbnails), `multerDigitalOcean` (disk, general uploads), `multerMemory` (memory storage, 25 MB limit — xlsx bulk imports) |
| `helpers/errorHandler.js` | Express error-handler middleware |
| `helpers/quote-access.js` | `vendorMayModifyQuote(userInfo, rfqVendor)` — ownership guard preventing a vendor from mutating another factory's quote by `quote_id` (see §11) |
| `helpers/rfqBulkImport.js` | Factory bulk-offer xlsx workflow: `generateTemplate(vendorName, customers)` → ExcelJS workbook buffer; `parseImport(buffer)` → `{rows, images}`; `getNextFoffPriceCodes(sql, count, Op)` → sequential `foff-XXXX` price code array |
| `helpers/rfqCopyMerchGroupGuard.js` | Guard: prevents copying an RFQ item into a closed/inactive merch group (uses `is_active` flag) |
| `helpers/rfqHistory.js` | Copy-lineage traversal via `rfqItem_copied_from_id`; used by `getRfqItemHistory` endpoint |
| `helpers/rfqStepDedupe.js` | Deduplication helper for RFQ step-change records |
| `helpers/utility.js` | Shared utility functions |
| `models/db.js` | Sequelize init + model registration + **all schema migrations** (`ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS`) |
| `models/db/` | 60+ Sequelize model files (one per table) |
| `models/lib.model.js` | Legacy domain-method module (`Customer` namespace — see §11) |
| `routes/index.js` | Root router — mounts all subrouters |
| `routes/lib.router.js` | **150+ endpoints** mounted at `/` — RFQ, vendor, product CRUD. Never read whole — grep or use offset/limit |
| `routes/admin.router.js` | `/api/admin` — app settings, section 301, HTS rates, Trump 2025, factories, impersonation |
| `routes/auth.router.js` | `/auth` — Azure SSO login |
| `routes/notification.router.js` | `/api/notifications` |
| `routes/user.router.js` | `/api/user` — profile, photo upload |
| `routes/openrouter.router.js` | `/api/openrouter` — AI proxy |
| `routes/cross.router.js` | `/api/cross` — CBP CROSS rulings proxy |
| `routes/comment.router.js` | `/api/comment` |
| `routes/customer.router.js` | `/api/customers` |
| `routes/grid.router.js` | `/api/grid` |
| `routes/health.router.js` | `/api/health` |
| `routes/digitalocean.router.js` | File storage endpoints mounted at `/` |
| `routes/` (others) | `itemSize`, `itemDepth`, `std`, `role`, `autofill` |
| `services/` | Business logic layer — Sequelize queries and domain operations |
| `controllers/` | Thin route handlers; delegate to services or `models/lib.model.js` |
| `tests/unit/` | Jest unit tests (227 tests across 19 suites, hermetic, runs in ~1 s) |
| `data/hts-rates.json` | Bundled USITC HTS schedule (~1 MB, ~13.6 k codes) — regenerated yearly by `scripts/fetch-hts-rates.js` |
| `scripts/fetch-hts-rates.js` | One-shot downloader to rebuild `data/hts-rates.json` from USITC |
| `docs/` | `architecture.md`, `configuration.md`, `development.md`, `deployment.md` |
| `HTS_RAG.md` | Comprehensive implementation plan for the future internal HTS RAG/precedent system; includes mandatory shadow-mode validation against legacy CROSS before any fast path |
| `.env.example` | Template for local `.env.<env-name>` files |
| `Dockerfile` | Single-stage Node production image |
| `cloudbuild.yaml` | Google Cloud Build CI/CD definition |
| `node_modules/` | Third-party packages — **never edit** |

---

## 4. The Prime Directive

The entire repo is custom code — there is no committed vendor framework directory analogous to the frontend's `src/@vex/`. Everything under the repo root is fair game **except**:

- `node_modules/` — never edit.
- `models/db/*.js` model files are effectively schema declarations. When you add a column to a model, you **must** also add the matching `ALTER TABLE … ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS` migration in `models/db.js` or the column will never exist in production. See §6 Decision Tree.
- `routes/lib.router.js` and `models/lib.model.js` are giant legacy files (150+ endpoints, ~3000 lines). **Add to them surgically rather than refactoring.** Any refactor that touches most of the file needs a coordinated effort — not a solo AI session.

---

## 5. Core modification inventory

No third-party or framework files outside our directories have been intentionally modified. There is no vendor layer to track here.

| File | Change made | Why | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| — | — | — | — |

---

## 6. Decision tree

**Add a new API endpoint:**
1. Choose the right router (`lib.router.js` for RFQ/product/vendor; a named router for anything else)
2. Add a thin handler in `controllers/`; keep business logic in `services/` or `models/lib.model.js`
3. Gate with `authRole([...roles])` from `helpers/auth.js`
4. Add a Jest test in `tests/unit/`
5. Document in `docs/architecture.md` § Routes

**Add a column to a database table:**
1. Add the field to the Sequelize model in `models/db/<Model>.js`
2. Add `sequelize.query(\`ALTER TABLE ${schema}"TableName" ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS "col_name" TYPE\`).catch(...)` inside `initialize()` in `models/db.js`. **If the column name is mixed-case (e.g. `rfqItem_price_sales_snapshots`), it MUST be double-quoted in the ALTER TABLE.** Sequelize quotes all identifiers, so an unquoted mixed-case name is folded to lowercase by Postgres and Sequelize never finds it — `findAll` then throws and the grid breaks (see §14, SYS-002).
3. Add a backfill query in the same block if needed (idempotent — guard with `WHERE col IS NULL`)
4. Add a regression assertion in `tests/unit/db.migration.test.js`
5. **Do not skip step 2.** `sequelize.sync()` does NOT alter existing tables. Missing this step causes "column does not exist" crashes in production (see §14, SYS-030).

**Add a new Sequelize model (new table):**
1. Create `models/db/<Model>.js` following the existing pattern
2. Register in `models/db/init-models.js`
3. Write a `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` migration in `models/db.js`

**Change auth or role logic:**
1. Auth middleware: `helpers/auth.js` (`authRole`, `getUserInfo`)
2. Role names come from the JWT payload: `admin`, `sales`, `designer`, `production`, `sourcing_manager`, `vendor`
3. User-specific permission reads: `GET /api/role/getUserRolePermissions/:userId` admits all logged-in app roles at the route layer, then `controllers/role.controller.js` enforces self-read unless the caller is `admin`, `production`, or `sourcing_manager`. Keep that controller check in sync with any route role changes.
4. Context (per-request user without prop-drilling): `helpers/context.middleware.js`
5. Do not add route-level auth bypass — all protected routes must use `authRole`

**Change admin impersonation:**
1. Endpoint: `controllers/admin.controller.js` `startImpersonation`, mounted by `routes/admin.router.js` at `POST /api/admin/impersonate/:userId`
2. Normal user rows resolve `:userId` against `users.id`
3. Visible factory rows send `{ targetType: 'factory' }` and resolve `:userId` against `Factory.id`; this is the primary Factories & Reps path because the frontend may not expose a master-detail expand button.
4. Nested rep rows, if shown, send `{ targetType: 'vendor' }` and resolve `:userId` against `vendor.vendor_id`
5. Vendor/factory impersonation tokens must include `role: 'vendor'`, `access`, `factory_id`, and `impersonated_by`. For visible factory rows, `factory_id` is `Factory.id` and `email` may be `null`; downstream vendor endpoints must not require `vendor_email`.
6. `Customer.getRFQStep` must fall back to the JWT `role` when no user/vendor row resolves, otherwise factory-row impersonation can crash Cloud Run with `Cannot read properties of null (reading 'level')`.
7. Add/update `tests/unit/admin.impersonation.test.js` and `tests/unit/rfqStep.vendor-token.test.js` when changing this flow

**Add a persistent app setting (survives redeploy):**
1. Use `services/app-setting.service.js` (`get(key)` / `set(key, value)`)
2. The `app_settings` table is the canonical key/value store (see §8)
3. For data that is static per calendar year and the same for every customer, prefer a bundled JSON file over an admin-managed upload (`data/hts-rates.json` is the model)

**Add or change a tariff lookup (Section 301 / MFN / Trump 2025):**
- Section 301: `helpers/section301.js` + `routes/admin.router.js`
- MFN base rate: `helpers/hts-rates.js` (read from `data/hts-rates.json`); re-run `scripts/fetch-hts-rates.js` yearly
- Trump 2025: `helpers/trump2025.js` + `app_settings['trump_2025_tariffs']`

**Refresh the bundled HTS schedule:**
```bash
node scripts/fetch-hts-rates.js
```
Run once per year when USITC publishes a new revision. Commit the updated `data/hts-rates.json`.

**Add an email notification:**
1. `helpers/brevo.js` — Brevo transactional email wrapper
2. `services/notification.service.js` — in-app notification store
3. `helpers/mention.js` — @mention pipeline (auto-invoked from `addComment`)

---

## 7. Task-to-file navigation map

| Task | Files to touch | Files not to touch |
|---|---|---|
| Add an RFQ/vendor/product endpoint | `routes/lib.router.js`, `controllers/main.controller.js` or `services/` | Other routers unless the feature clearly belongs elsewhere |
| Add an admin endpoint | `routes/admin.router.js`, `controllers/admin.controller.js` | `lib.router.js` |
| Change admin impersonation | `controllers/admin.controller.js`, `models/lib.model.js` if vendor-token identity is affected, `routes/admin.router.js`, `tests/unit/admin.impersonation.test.js`, `tests/unit/rfqStep.vendor-token.test.js`, `docs/architecture.md` | `helpers/auth.js` unless role verification itself changes |
| Add/change a DB column | `models/db/<Model>.js`, `models/db.js` (migration), `tests/unit/db.migration.test.js` | Any other file unless the column needs a new service method |
| Add a tariff helper | `helpers/<name>.js`, corresponding test in `tests/unit/` | Route files (add endpoint separately) |
| Change auth pipeline | `helpers/auth.js`, `index.js` (if the middleware swap changes) | Route files (auth is middleware, not per-route logic) |
| Change file storage behaviour | `helpers/digitalocean.js`, `routes/digitalocean.router.js` | `helpers/multer.js` (unless upload constraints change) |

## RFQ Buyer Target edit gate fix — **COMMITTED & PUSHED, PR open** (updated 2026-07-07)

### 1. What this application is

Designflow is POP Creations' product-lifecycle-management web application. The Angular frontend in
`C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-frontend` is used by sourcing, sales, design, licensing, production, and
factory/vendor users to manage RFQs, item-library records, licensing/production tracking, and related
workflow screens. This repo is the frontend only; the main PLM API lives in the sibling
`designflow-backend` service. The RFQ grid is business-critical because it stores vendor prices, buyer
targets, landed-cost inputs, sell-price calculations, and margin calculations used for quoting retailers.

Current working branch for this repo is `sandbox-albert`, pushed to
`origin/sandbox-albert`, with PR `sandbox-albert -> develop` open:
https://github.com/popcre/designflow-frontend/pull/141.

### 2. What we set out to do this session, and why

Albert reported that the new RFQ `"Buyer Target"` and `"Buyer Margin %"` fields were "dead" because users
could not type into either of them. These fields were part of the 16-column RFQ buyer-margin feature:
eight editable `"* Buyer Target"` dollar fields and eight calculated `"* Buyer Margin %"` fields. The
business goal is for permitted users to type a buyer target price on POP/Spruce RFQ rows, then have the
corresponding buyer margin percentage calculate from the existing RFQ cost basis without altering the
sourcing team's original sell-price/margin fields.

### 3. Current state

The frontend code fix is committed and pushed:

- Commit: `5c3f6dcd642cc336e232ecb454363970ce350446`

- Branch: `sandbox-albert`

  https://github.com/popcre/designflow-frontend/actions/runs/28887656252

Files changed:

- `src/app/pages/rfq/helpers/buyer-margin-visibility.ts`
- `src/app/pages/rfq/helpers/buyer-margin-visibility.spec.ts`

Behavior now:

- `"Buyer Target"` editability recognizes RFQ rows whose division arrives as:
  - `Div: "Spruce Lic"`, `Div: "Spruce non-Lic"`, or `Div: "POP Lic"`
  - `Div: { title: ... }`
  - `Div: { divCode_id: 1 | 8 | 9 }`
  - `Div: { id: 1 | 8 | 9 }`

- POP Lic (`1`), Spruce Lic (`8`), and Spruce non-Lic (`9`) are in buyer-target scope.

  `rfq.component.ts` calling `equation.recalculate_Buyer_Margins(...)`. This is the existing spec in

### 4. Everything we tried that did NOT work

Initial suspicion was that both `"Buyer Target"` and `"Buyer Margin %"` were editable fields and that the grid might be
blocking both. Reading `docs/rfq-buyer-margin-plan.md` and the RFQ grid config showed that this was only partly true:
`"Buyer Target"` should be editable, but `"Buyer Margin %"` is deliberately read-only because it is a calculated result.
Changing `"Buyer Margin %"` to editable would have contradicted the shipped feature design.

The first implementation pass hardened the division helper to read `Div`, lower-case `div`,
`rfqItem_divCode_id_fk`, and `div_code_fk`. The new focused test then failed for
`isBuyerScopeRow({ rfqItem_divCode_id_fk: 8 })`: the helper found the raw alias value but only treated raw
`div_code_fk` as a numeric code. The fix was to treat the raw division value itself as the candidate code
when the value is not an object. After that, the focused helper test passed.

The first `git status` was run from `C:\repos\dflow plm`, which is only the parent folder for the six dflow repos,
not a git repository. The actual repo for this fix is `C:\repos\dflow plm\designflow-frontend`.

### 5. Root causes and key findings

Root cause: `src/app/pages/rfq/helpers/buyer-margin-visibility.ts` originally checked only
`data['Div']` and only by matching division names that include `"spruce"` or `"pop"`. Some RFQ grid data paths carry
division data under aliases or as numeric ids instead of the literal `Div` display field. When AG Grid asked whether
a Buyer Target cell was editable, `isBuyerScopeRow(params.data)` returned `false`, so AG Grid never opened the numeric
editor.

Key code locations:

- `src/app/pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts` around the Buyer Target setup:
  `colHeaders[i].editable = (params) => isBuyerScopeRow(params?.data);`
- `src/app/pages/rfq/helpers/buyer-margin-visibility.ts` lines 26-63 after the fix:
  `getDivisionValue(...)`, `getDivisionCodeId(...)`, `isSpruceRow(...)`, `isPopRow(...)`,
  and `isBuyerScopeRow(...)` now handle the known RFQ shapes.
- `src/app/helpers/ag-grid/rfq-grid/rfq.grid.config.ts` defines the buyer columns. The Buyer Margin columns have
  `editable: false` by design; the Buyer Target columns inherit editability and then get row-level gating in
  `rfq.component.ts`.

Non-obvious finding: Albert's phrase "can't type anything into either of them" sounds like two editable fields, but
the intended workflow is "type Buyer Target, read calculated Buyer Margin %." If the UI still feels wrong to Albert,
clarify whether he now wants Buyer Margin % to be manually overrideable; that is a product change, not a bug fix.

## Performance follow-ups — **DEFERRED, earmarked for `albert-2sandbox` branch** (added 2026-06-22)

These came out of the 2026-06-22 page-load performance review. The three lower-risk
items (RFQ init `forkJoin`, reference-data caching, per-row-expansion caching) were
**already implemented** on the working branch — see `docs/performance-caching.md`.
The items below were intentionally **not** done yet; do them on `albert-2sandbox`.

### A. Server-side pagination for the RFQ `getAll` query — **DESIGNED (2026-06-23), pending developer review**

> 📄 **Full implementation design: [`docs/rfq-ssrm-migration-plan.md`](docs/rfq-ssrm-migration-plan.md)** —
> a complete cross-repo (backend + frontend) SSRM-with-server-side-grouping plan with the field
> map, filter/sort translators, Res + merch-group filterability, group/leaf query design,
> full-dataset export, file list, test plan, risks, and open questions for the reviewer.
> Product-owner decisions baked in: keep server-side grouping; Res + merch-groups must be
> filterable (carton dims need not be); export the full filtered set. **Not yet implemented** —
> review the doc before building; ship behind the dual-endpoint so `/getAllItem` stays as rollback.

**Problem:** the RFQ grid's backend `getAll` (`designflow-backend/models/lib.model.js`,
`Customer.getAll`) does an unbounded `findAll` across ~10 joined tables and returns
**all** rows (currently 3000+). It's the heaviest query behind the page.

**⚠️ The critical caveat (why this is deferred):** the RFQ grid currently uses AG
Grid's **client-side row model** — all rows are in the browser, so filtering and
sorting work across the *entire* dataset for free. If you naïvely add `LIMIT/OFFSET`
to the backend **without** also moving to AG Grid's **server-side row model**, then
filter/sort will only act on the one page currently loaded — i.e. **filtering would
silently stop working across all 3000+ rows.** That is a functional regression, not
just a perf change.

**Correct approach (the whole job):**
1. Switch the RFQ grid to the server-side row model (SSRM) — see how
   `itemLibrary` / `prod_tracking` already do this (they use SSRM today).
2. Push **filtering, sorting, and pagination to the backend** so they operate over
   the full set, and have `getAll` accept `startRow/endRow/filterModel/sortModel`.
3. Return a total row count for the grid.
4. Re-verify: Saved Views, the Division logic, default Step filter, master/detail
   expansion, export (must export the full filtered set, not just the loaded page),
   and cell notes.

**Do not ship a bare `LIMIT` on `getAll`.** Either do the full SSRM migration or leave it.

### B. Adopt `ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush` + precompute template getters

**Problem:** only ~8 of ~278 components use OnPush. Grid pages (rfq, itemLibrary,
prod_tracking, etc.) use default change detection with method/getter bindings that
re-run every CD cycle (e.g. `valueGetter` doing `allMerchGroups.find(...)` per cell,
template getters that rebuild arrays).

**Approach (incremental, per-component — do NOT flip globally):**
- Convert one heavy component at a time to `OnPush`; drive updates through the
  already-injected `ChangeDetectorRef` (`markForCheck()`), and use immutable updates
  (new array/object references) instead of in-place mutation.
- Precompute template getter results into fields when the source data changes.
- **Risk:** OnPush changes *when* the view updates — anything relying on mutate-in-place
  will silently stop refreshing. Each component needs manual testing after conversion.

### C. N+1 / row-by-row writes → `bulkCreate` — **AUDIT-FIRST process defined (2026-06-23)**

> 📄 **Audit template + staged process + hotspot inventory:
> [`designflow-data-syncing/docs/bulkcreate-migration-audit.md`](../designflow-data-syncing/docs/bulkcreate-migration-audit.md)**
> (lives in the repo where the work happens). It defines a per-hotspot fill-in audit checklist and a
> staged process: **(0) fix error visibility → (1) per-hotspot audit incl. the conditional-update
> decision → (2) verify/add the DB unique constraint → (3) baseline + tests + scheduler validation
> in non-prod → (4) chunked `bulkCreate` in a transaction → (5) parity check → next service.**
> Migrate **one service at a time**; first service is a pattern-setter (merch-group sync), NOT the
> riskiest (`getProdOrderDetail`).

This is primarily a **backend / sync-service** concern, listed here so the frontend
handoff points at it. Row-by-row `INSERT`/`UPDATE` loops (often with a per-row
`SELECT` first) should become batched `bulkCreate(rows, { updateOnDuplicate: [...] })`
or batched `findAll({ where: { [Op.in]: keys } })`. Hotspots identified:
`designflow-data-syncing` sync loops (e.g. `getProdOrderDetail`, merch-group sync,
prepack associations) and `designflow-tracking` `Box.addSamples`.

**Why "investigate further", not "just do it":**
- Each site needs a real **unique constraint** + an explicit `updateOnDuplicate`
  column list — wrong list either clobbers kept data or silently skips updates.
- Some loops do **conditional** updates ("only if incoming `modTime` is newer") that
  `updateOnDuplicate` can't express without a SQL `CASE`/literal — naive batching can
  resurrect stale data.
- Per-row **hooks/side-effects** (Teams notifications, audit writes) are skipped by
  `bulkCreate` unless `individualHooks: true` (which removes most of the speed gain).
- Watch Postgres bound-parameter limits (~65k) → cap batch size; wrap multi-step
  writes in a transaction for atomicity.

Action: audit each hotspot for the four points above, then migrate service-by-service
with the upsert keys verified against existing data first.

## Factory bulk offer upload — **IMPLEMENTED, NOT YET COMMITTED** (updated 2026-06-11)

A vendor-only "Upload Offers" feature was fully implemented across both `designflow-frontend` and `designflow-backend` in this session. All code sits as unstaged changes on branch `sandbox-albert` in both repos.

### What it does

Factory/vendor users see an **Upload Offers** button (`@if (role === 'vendor')`) in the RFQ toolbar. Clicking it opens a 3-stage dialog:

1. **Form** — download a pre-formatted `.xlsx` template (pre-filled with factory name, date, Customer dropdown, Price Terms dropdown, 50 tall data rows with yellow image cells); name the RFQ group; drop-zone to select the completed file
2. **Uploading** — spinner while the API processes the file
3. **Result** — list of created items (price code + description) and any row-level errors (bilingual English · Chinese)

On the backend, the import creates:
- One `RFQGroup` (named by the factory)
- One `RFQItem` per non-empty data row — price code `foff-XXXX` (global sequential; padded to 4 digits)
- One `RFQVendor` quote record per row (linked to the uploading factory's `factory_id` from JWT; `req_status = 2` = quoted; lands at @factories step)
- Embedded product images extracted from the xlsx and uploaded to DigitalOcean Spaces via `uploadBufferToSpaces`

## 1. Notification trigger points (backend — designflow-backend)

Nothing fires `createNotification()` on business events. The notification system exists
(routes, service, frontend bell/badge) but no triggers have been wired.

**Pattern to follow** (from the existing @mention handler):

```js
setImmediate(async () => {
  try {
    await NotificationService.createManyNotifications(targets, {
      type: 'task_assignment',  // or 'status_change', 'approval'
      event: 'assigned',
      title: 'Task assigned',
      message: `${authorName} assigned a task to you`,
    });
  } catch {}
});
```

**Where to wire it:**
- Task assignment: `services/task.service.js` or wherever `createTask` / `assignTask` lives
- RFQ status change: `routes/lib.router.js` PATCH handler for `rfq_status`
- Licensing approval: `routes/lib.router.js` PATCH handler for `licensing_status`

**Frontend icon map already in place** (`notification.router.ts` maps `type` → icon/color):
| `type` | Icon |
|---|---|
| `mention` | alternate_email |
| `task_assignment` | assignment_ind |
| `approval` | check_circle |
| anything else | notifications |

---

## 3. RAG step 1: keyword search over hts-rates.json (backend — designflow-backend)

Add a `/api/admin/hts-rates/search?desc={text}` endpoint that does a simple keyword match
over description fields in `data/hts-rates.json`. Inject the top 5 matches into the AI's
initial message as "Potentially relevant HTS headings." Zero new infrastructure — uses the
already-bundled USITC JSON file.

**Why it matters:** The system prompt covers 9 chapters. For products outside those chapters,
the model falls back on training data. A simple keyword search would inject the exact USITC
subheadings the model should consider, improving accuracy on edge-case products.

**Implementation sketch:**
- Backend: `helpers/hts-rates.js` — add `searchByDescription(text, limit = 5)` that filters
  `hts-rates.json` entries whose `description` field contains any token from `text`.
- Route: `routes/admin.router.js` — `GET /api/admin/hts-rates/search?desc={text}`
- Frontend: `ai-settings.service.ts` — add `searchHtsRates(desc: string)` method
- Inject in `initAiSession()` alongside CROSS results (non-fatal if empty or missing)

---

## Licensing tracking ↔ poppim PM integration — Phase-0 bridge (updated 2026-06-14)

**Status:** shared-key verification **DONE (GO, 99.3%)**; Phase-0 bridge **designed, not yet built**.

**Context:** See `docs/licensing-tracking-poppim-integration.md` (analysis + verified result) and
`docs/licensing-poppim-phase0-bridge.md` (the build spec). Albert's direction (2026-06-13): DesignFlow
stays the system of record for licensing approval; poppim (Directus app at `pm.designflow.app`) consumes a
**read-only** copy.

**Done (this session):**
- Confirmed both data models; the two run on separate clouds/DBs (API↔API, no shared DB).
- **Verified the join key (GO):** DesignFlow `item_num_id` ⇄ the item-number prefix of poppim
  `product.name` (NOT `product.code`). **9,224 / 9,293 (99.3%)** poppim item-nos match DesignFlow; misses
  are truncated codes. Matches are almost entirely licensed (div 1/2/8). Key is **not** unique on
  `item_num_id` alone → use `(item_num_id, compan_code_fk, div_code_fk)`.
- Wrote the Phase-0 bridge spec (read-only, one-way DesignFlow→poppim status mirror).

**Next action (build the bridge — see `docs/licensing-poppim-phase0-bridge.md`):**
1. Collect the 4 open inputs (spec §8): export the poppim `stage` collection; run
   `SELECT id, phase, status, "order" FROM designflow."LicenseFeedBacks" ORDER BY "order";`; obtain a
   Directus service-account write token; pick hosting (Cloud Run job vs Directus Flow).
2. Add the read-only `df_*` fields + backfill `product_link.linked_external_id` in the `u2giants/directus`
   schema repo.
3. Build the bulk read endpoint on DesignFlow (`/lic/status-export`) and the idempotent sync job.
4. Dry-run/log-only one cycle, spot-check 20 products, then enable writes.

**Risks / watchouts:**
- Key needs the `(item_num_id + company + division)` composite (`item_num_id` alone has ~1,500 DF
  collisions; 367 poppim products share an item-no). `compan_code_fk` is often NULL — tolerate it.
- ~42% of poppim "products" are non-product ClickUp rows (comments / routing notes / presentations) — the
  bridge must filter these, not treat every poppim product row as a SKU.
- The bridge is **API↔API across GCP↔Coolify**; there is no shared DB. Do not assume a SQL join is possible.

---

## RFQ session 2026-06-15 — redesign reverted; backend develop-sync reverted

Status: done (everything reverted/shipped); a future develop re-sync needs care.

- All RFQ polish + redesign work this session was reverted on `sandbox-albert` (frontend revert commit `268e04ef`). It was reverted (not force-removed) to preserve the parallel Item Detail redesign commit (`c9b22f95`) that landed on top.
- The **only kept change** is the `index.html` cache fix (`7e9ca2d7`, `nginx.conf`) — see `AGENTS.md §14` and `docs/deployment.md → Static asset caching`.
- The backend `develop → sandbox-albert` merge was also reverted (`designflow-backend` `087fdc5`). **Re-syncing develop later needs care:** reverting a *merge commit* means a plain `git merge develop` won't re-bring it — see `designflow-backend/AGENTS.md §14`. Land the cache fix to prod first so the lazy-loading rollout doesn't re-break stale browsers.
- If the RFQ visual redesign is revisited, it MUST go through the AG Grid Theming API, not CSS overrides — see `AGENTS.md §11 "The RFQ grid uses AG Grid's Theming API"`. A corrected design brief was drafted in-session but not committed.

---

Delete this file once all items above are resolved.

payload.message: One backend edge case surfaced in review before I move on: group-level filtering needs the same lookup joins as leaf rows, otherwise filters like Status or Customer can generate SQL aliases that are not joined. content.text: One backend edge case surfaced in review before I move on: group-level filtering needs the same lookup joins as leaf rows, otherwise filters like Status or Customer can generate SQL aliases that are not joined. I’m tightening the group query builder to include lookup joins with no selected attributes.

# Browser

Use this skill for browser automation tasks such as inspecting pages, navigating, testing local apps, clicking, typing, taking screenshots, and reading visible page state.

If this plugin is listed as available in the session, treat that as mandatory reading before browser work. Open and follow this skill before saying that Browser is unavailable and before falling back to standalone Playwright or Computer Use.

Do not skip this skill just because Computer Use MCP tool calls are directly visible or appear easier to invoke. The presence of Computer Use tools is not evidence that Computer Use is the preferred browser surface.

## Setup Documentation

Use `await agent.documentation.get("bootstrap-troubleshooting")` when browser setup succeeds but discovery or selection fails.

## Bootstrap

These setup details are internal. User-facing progress updates should be less technical in nature. Never mention `Node REPL`, `node_repl`, `REPL`, JavaScript sessions, module exports, reading documentation, or loading instructions unless a user is asking for that exact information. If setup or recovery is needed, describe it naturally as connecting to the browser or retrying the browser connection.

The `browser-client` module is the core entry point for browser use, and is available under `scripts/browser-client.mjs` in this plugin's root directory. ALWAYS import it using an absolute path. IMPORTANT: If this path cannot be found, stop and report that this plugin is missing `scripts/browser-client.mjs`. NEVER use the built in `browser-client` library.

Run browser setup code through the Node REPL `js` tool. In this environment the callable tool id typically appears as `mcp__node_repl__js`. If it is not already available, use tool discovery for `node_repl js` without setting a result limit. You need the `js` execution tool: `js_reset` only clears state, and `js_add_node_module_dir` only changes package resolution. Do not call either helper while trying to expose `js`. If `js` is still not available, search again for `node_repl js` with `limit: 10`.

Initialize the runtime once per fresh Node session, select the in-app browser, and immediately read its complete documentation:

```js
if (globalThis.agent?.browsers == null) {
  const { setupBrowserRuntime } = await import("<plugin root>/scripts/browser-client.mjs");
  await setupBrowserRuntime({ globals: globalThis });
}
globalThis.browser = await agent.browsers.get("iab");
nodeRepl.write(await browser.documentation());
```

If setup succeeds but browser discovery or selection fails, read `await agent.documentation.get("bootstrap-troubleshooting")` before resetting the JavaScript session or trying another browser-control mechanism.

Use the browser bound to `browser` for tasks in this skill. Do not switch to another browser-control mechanism to work around authentication. If sign-in blocks requested navigation and the browser documentation does not provide a supported authentication flow, ask the user to sign in in the in-app browser and tell you when it is ready.

The ability to interact directly with the browser is exposed through the `browser-client` runtime via the `agent.browsers.*` API. Before trying to interact with it, you MUST emit and read the complete documentation returned by `await browser.documentation()` in one go. For the initial documentation read, run the exact direct call `nodeRepl.write(await browser.documentation());` shown above. Do not assign the documentation to a variable, inspect its length, slice it, truncate it, summarize it, or emit only an excerpt. Do not proactively split the documentation into pages or chunks. Only if the tool output itself explicitly reports that it was truncated may you emit and read smaller chunks until you have read the documentation in its entirety.

Only the Node REPL `js` tool (`mcp__node_repl__js`) can be used to control the in-app browser. Do not use external MCP browser-control tools, separate browser automation servers, or other browser skills for this surface. References to Playwright mean the in-skill `tab.playwright` API after browser-client setup.
